Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation

Book overview and step study-note index for Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation.

Book notes

Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation

Study notes on Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation (Patton 2012). These step-level notes are generated from source-linked MCP research-library records and are intended for study, comparison, and later refinement.

19 intro/step/conclusion notes 2012 Source status: needs_review MCP evaluation-texts

Synthesis

Core Concepts

Recurring ideas and distinctions used across these step-level notes.

Map

Chapter Map

Full reading route for the introduction, 17 U-FE steps, and conclusion.

Practice

Practice Implications

Practical utilization-focused evaluation habits drawn from the full step set.

Source

2012Book overviewStep route

This index summarises generated study notes for Michael Quinn Patton’s Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation (Patton 2012). The generated pages use selected MCP research-library retrieval records and compact evidence summaries.

Core Argument

Utilization-focused evaluation designs evaluation around intended use by intended users, treating readiness, users, uses, questions, methods, interpretation, reporting, follow-up, and metaevaluation as an interacting system rather than a linear technical sequence.

Step-Level Notes

  • Introduction, Overview, and Context - source pages 27-37
  • Step 1: Assess and Build Program and Organizational Readiness for Utilization-Focused Evaluation - source pages 39-59
  • Step 2: Assess and Enhance Evaluator Readiness and Competence to Undertake a Utilization-Focused Evaluation - source pages 60-83
  • Step 3: Identify, Organize, and Engage Primary Intended Users: The Personal Factor - source pages 84-109
  • Step 4: Situation Analysis Conducted Jointly With Primary Intended Users - source pages 110-135
  • Step 5: Identify and Prioritize Primary Intended Uses by Determining Priority Purposes - source pages 136-162
  • Step 6: Consider and Build in Process Uses if and as Appropriate - source pages 163-190
  • Step 7: Focus Priority Evaluation Questions - source pages 192-212
  • Step 8: Check That Fundamental Areas for Evaluation Inquiry Are Being Adequately Addressed: Implementation, Outcomes, and Attribution Questions - source pages 213-251
  • Step 9: Determine What Intervention Model or Theory of Change Is Being Evaluated - source pages 252-282
  • Step 10: Negotiate Appropriate Methods to Generate Credible Findings That Support Intended Use by Intended Users - source pages 283-306
  • Step 11: Make Sure Intended Users Understand Potential Methods Controversies and Their Implications - source pages 307-330
  • Step 12: Simulate Use of Findings: Evaluation’s Equivalent of a Dress Rehearsal - source pages 331-348
  • Step 13: Gather Data With Ongoing Attention to Use - source pages 349-360
  • Step 14: Organize and Present the Data for Interpretation and Use by Primary Intended Users: Analysis, Interpretation, Judgment, and Recommendations - source pages 361-387
  • Step 15: Prepare an Evaluation Report to Facilitate Use and Disseminate Significant Findings to Expand Influence - source pages 388-400
  • Step 16: Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use - source pages 401-410
  • Step 17: Metaevaluation of Use: Be Accountable, Learn, and Improve - source pages 411-420
  • Summary and Conclusion - source pages 426-449

Legacy Extracts

  • Utilization-Focused Evaluation Overview
  • The 17-Step Utilization-Focused Checklist
  • Negotiating Methods for Intended Use

References

Patton, Michael Quinn. 2012. Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation. SAGE Publications.
